---
title: "Git Push/Pull Bridge for PHI-Constrained Work"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
---

```{r}
#| include: false
if (requireNamespace("framework", quietly = TRUE)) {
  framework::scaffold()
} else {
  if (file.exists("../../scaffold.R")) source("../../scaffold.R")
}
```

## The idea

Code can cross boundaries. Data can't. Git gives us a clean way to enforce that.

Work locally with synthetic or public data, push the code, pull it into the restricted environment, and run it there against the real thing. The repo history becomes your audit trail for what moved and when.

## Local side

```bash
#| eval: false
git checkout -b workflow-demo
git status
git add demos/5-git-push-pull/
git commit -m "Add session 3 workflow notebooks"
git push -u origin workflow-demo
```

## Restricted side

```bash
#| eval: false
git fetch origin
git checkout workflow-demo
git pull
quarto render demos/7-phi-two-machine/phi-two-machine-synthetic-simulated.qmd
```

## `.gitignore` matters here

Your `.gitignore` should block data, credentials, and generated artifacts from ever reaching the remote. It also cuts down on the noise agents wade through when searching files.

This repo already ignores `util_docs/`, `.env`, and `course_docs/readings/*.pdf`.

## Discussion

If the same code works locally but fails in restricted compute, what should we check first?
